title: Gingerbread house
subtitle: Crisp, spiced gingerbread panels for building and decorating
excerpt: A structured, spiced gingerbread designed for building a decorated house rather than eating straight from the tin. The dough is firmer and drier than a standard gingerbread biscuit, with a low spreading factor and good structural strength once cooled. Royal icing acts as both mortar and decoration. A full house requires two side panels, two roof panels, a front, and a back; all are cut from templates before baking.
sourceNotes: Gingerbread houses (lebkuchenhaus or pepparkakor-hus) have roots in German and Scandinavian baking traditions documented from the seventeenth century onward. The Brothers Grimm fairy tale Hansel and Gretel (first published 1812, public domain and available at Project Gutenberg) describes an edible house of gingerbread, reflecting a pre-existing tradition. Recipes for structural gingerbread panels suitable for house-building appear in German and Scandinavian domestic baking guides published before 1928.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 full gingerbread house (decorative; panels serve 10 to 12 when dismantled)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the panels up to a week ahead and store flat in an airtight container. Assemble and decorate the day before display. The assembled house keeps at room temperature for up to 10 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 150°C. Lower temperature than decorative biscuits to ensure even colour without scorching the thin edges.

recipeTools.notes: For royal icing assembly and decoration.

glossaryTerms.definition: Using royal icing (icing sugar beaten with egg white to a stiff, smooth paste) as the adhesive for assembling a gingerbread house. The icing sets hard within 10 to 15 minutes, so panels must be held or propped in place until the join is firm. Apply the icing thickly to the joint edges and work in sections: attach the front and back to the base, wait for them to set, then add the side walls, wait again, then add the roof. Rush the assembly and the structure will lean or collapse. The icing must be stiff enough to hold its shape when piped.

content.text: Building a gingerbread house is a construction project as much as a baking one. Allow time for the panels to cool completely before assembly, and assemble in stages, letting each section of
set firm before adding the next. A house assembled in one rush tends to lean, split at the joins, or collapse before the icing sets.

Beat the butter and dark brown sugar together until combined. Beat in the golden syrup and egg. Stir in the flour, spices, bicarbonate of soda, and salt until a firm dough forms. Divide into two discs, wrap, and refrigerate for 1 hour.

Draw or print house templates on cardboard and cut out. Heat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan). Roll the chilled dough to 4 to 5 mm thick on a lightly floured surface. Lay templates on the dough and cut around them with a sharp knife. Transfer to lined baking trays. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes per batch until even in colour and just firm; they harden further on cooling. Cool flat on a rack. Re-cut any panels that spread out of shape while still warm.

Beat the egg whites with the lemon juice until frothy. Add the icing sugar and beat until thick, smooth, and holds stiff peaks. Transfer to a piping bag. Pipe a thick line of icing along the joins and assemble the house in stages, holding or propping each section until it sets (10 to 15 minutes per join). Decorate the assembled house with remaining icing and sweets.
